expectations.text: hook.md commits to AT LEAST ONE of these named hook types in its opening 80 words, identifiable by structural pattern: (a) MISCONCEPTION — first 2 sentences contain a stated conventional view followed by a literal reversal word ('but', 'however', 'actually', 'except', 'the truth is', 'turns out'); (b) IN-MEDIAS-RES — opens with a specific time marker (e.g. '02:47 AM', 'last Tuesday', 'in Q3 2024') AND a specific actor or place in the same or adjacent sentence; (c) FAILED-EXPECTATION — opens with at least 3 sentences forming a 'we did X / we expected Y / we got Z' pattern (3 short clauses, comma- or period-separated, containing 'expected'/'thought'/'assumed' contrasted with 'instead'/'actually'/'turned out'); (d) SURPRISING-FACT — opens with a number paired with a 'not X, not Y' explicit comparison pattern; (e) ARTIFACT-LED — first 50 words contain a literal code block, terminal-output snippet, or specific quoted error message. A generic essay opener about CI in general does NOT satisfy any of these.
expectations.evidence: Opening two sentences: 'Your engineers aren't waiting for code review. They're waiting for CI.' This creates a contrast effect but uses NO literal reversal word from the required list ('but', 'however', 'actually', 'except', 'the truth is', 'turns out'). (a) MISCONCEPTION fails on this criterion — the conventional view is implied through negation ('aren't'), not stated then reversed with an explicit word. (b) No specific time marker present. (c) No 3-clause 'we did X / expected Y / got Z' structure. (d) Hook does not open with a number + 'not X, not Y' comparison. (e) No code block or quoted error message in first 50 words. The opener is a clean contrast pivot but does not satisfy any named structural pattern.

expectations.text: hook.md contains AT LEAST TWO distinct specific numbers paired in the same paragraph — must be two genuinely different quantitative measures (e.g. duration + frequency: '12 min × 80 PRs/week'; cost + timeframe: '$200K/quarter for 18 months'; count + percentage: '47 services with 80% test coverage'). Two instances of the same measure (e.g. two durations '12 minutes' and '15 minutes') count as ONE distinct measure. Baseline hooks typically include one number; the skill explicitly teaches stacking compound concreteness.
expectations.evidence: First paragraph: 'That 18-minute pipeline isn't a minor inconvenience — at 200 engineers running three builds a day, it's over 1,800 hours of blocked work every week.' Contains: (1) 18 minutes — a duration measure; (2) 200 engineers — a headcount/count measure; (3) three builds/day — a frequency measure; (4) 1,800 hours — a volume/aggregate measure. Multiple pairs of distinct quantitative measures appear in the same paragraph, satisfying the requirement of at least two genuinely different measures.

expectations.text: hook.md respects Sugarman slippery-slide sentence discipline — must satisfy ALL THREE: (a) the FIRST sentence is ≤25 words, (b) NO single sentence in the hook exceeds 35 words, (c) at least ONE sentence in the hook is ≤8 words (a punch sentence used as a pivot). Baseline hooks tend to open with a 25-40 word balanced sentence and avoid short-punch sentences.
expectations.evidence: Sentence word counts: (1) 'Your engineers aren't waiting for code review.' = 7 words [≤25 ✓]; (2) 'They're waiting for CI.' = 4 words [punch sentence ≤8 ✓]; (3) 'That 18-minute pipeline isn't a minor inconvenience — at 200 engineers running three builds a day, it's over 1,800 hours of blocked work every week.' = ~24 words [≤35 ✓]; (4) 'That's a full engineering team doing nothing but watching progress bars.' = 11 words; (5) 'The frustrating part: most of that time isn't doing anything useful.' = 11 words; (6) 'It's re-running tests that already passed, downloading dependencies that haven't changed, and serializing jobs that could run in parallel.' = 19 words; (7) 'The root causes aren't mysterious — they're structural, and they compound as your team scales.' = 14 words; (8) 'What felt acceptable at 30 engineers becomes a productivity tax at 150.' = 12 words; (9) 'This post covers the patterns that slow down CI at growth-stage companies and the fixes that actually move the number — without a platform rewrite.' = ~24 words. All three Sugarman criteria satisfied: (a) first sentence = 7 words ≤25; (b) no sentence exceeds 35 words; (c) 'They're waiting for CI.' = 4 words satisfies ≤8 punch sentence.

claims.claim: Opens with a concrete cost calculation to grab attention
claims.evidence: 'That 18-minute pipeline ... at 200 engineers running three builds a day, it's over 1,800 hours of blocked work every week' — genuine multiplication-based cost calculation
Paragraph 2 lists: re-running tests that already passed, downloading unchanged dependencies, serializing parallelizable jobs — three structural causes named

claims.claim: Ends with a clear promise — no platform rewrite required
claims.evidence: Final sentence: 'the fixes that actually move the number — without a platform rewrite'
Word count of hook content is approximately 126 words across three paragraphs

suggestions.assertion: hook.md commits to AT LEAST ONE of these named hook types in its opening 80 words...
suggestions.reason: The without_skill executor produced a clean two-sentence contrast opener ('not A, but B') that intuitively reads as MISCONCEPTION-style but lacks the explicit reversal word the assertion requires. This is a meaningful discriminator — the skill should teach writers to include the explicit pivot word. However, consider whether a close-but-not-quite result that fools human readers should genuinely fail. The current binary (literal reversal word or not) is strict but well-reasoned.

No assertion verifies the hook's topic relevance — a hook about an entirely unrelated subject that still hit all structural patterns would PASS all three expectations. Adding an assertion that the hook is clearly about CI/CD pipeline performance would prevent a structurally correct but topic-drifted output from passing.

eval_feedback.overall: Assertions are well-constructed and discriminating. Expectation 1 correctly catches that the without_skill version produces intuitive contrast but not named structural technique. Expectation 2 and 3 are precise and check genuinely skill-taught behaviors. Minor gap: no topic-relevance guard.
